definition: A critical conceptual unit within the top term domain of the Exegesis. Mentions: 293.
interpretive_note: Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677) profoundly influenced PKD's understanding of the relationship between God and the universe. Spinoza's doctrine of *Deus sive Natura* (God or Nature) argued that there is only one infinite substance, and everything that exists is a modification of that substance. Dick adopted Spinozist pantheism (later refined into acosmic panentheism) to argue that the universe is not something created by God from the outside, but is rather an extension of God's own being. In the Exegesis, Dick frequently references Spinoza's concept of viewing things *sub specie aeternitatis* (under the aspect of eternity). To see the world *sub specie aeternitatis* is to overcome the illusion of time and subjective fragmentation, perceiving all events and entities as necessary, rational components of the single infinite divine mind, which perfectly paralleled Dick's own glimpses of reality behind the veil of maya.
